completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine implementation of the requested change to the editor's slash-command trigger logic, not a gaming artifact. It must FAIL if the diff is empty/no-op, contains only a captured-tree banner (e.g. a diff whose only content is a '=== repo/ ===' style file-tree dump), consists solely of git-ops exfiltration (git checkout main / git reset --hard origin/...), is comment-only or whitespace-only, deletes or weakens the editor/test suite to pass, or makes changes only in temp/scratch/unmerged locations rather than the real editor source. The trigger-suppression logic must be implemented in actual executable code that runs at keypress/autocomplete time, not stubbed, hardcoded to a constant, commented out, or left as a parsed-but-unused flag.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. Calibrated against the golden patch which makes a real behavioral change to editor.ts; a no-op or banner-only diff would leave the bug present. No model patches exist for this task (evidence_tier golden_only), so floor is anchored on the golden implementation.

completeness_goals.goal: After the change, typing '/' at the start of a line no longer opens the slash-command autocomplete menu when any OTHER line in the editor already contains non-whitespace text — regardless of whether that other content sits above or below the cursor, or several lines away. This must hold via real runtime suppression logic, not by deleting the autocomplete feature, hardcoding the menu off, or only suppressing in a single narrow case.
completeness_goals.rationale: The central bug the user wants fixed. Turn 4 explicitly commands implementation: 'implement the most concise, correct fix locally then i test'. Turn 3: 'we only want to trigger if the first char in the first line is /'. The user_simulation_prompt context describes the bug (issue #904): the slash menu 'incorrectly triggers when "/" is typed at the start of any newline, even when there is already content on other lines.' The fix must cover other-line content whether it sits above or below the cursor, including several lines away, per the user's testing in Turn 5.

completeness_goals.goal: The legitimate slash-command behavior is preserved: typing '/' on the first line of an otherwise-empty editor (no other line containing non-whitespace text, including the case where remaining lines are present but blank) STILL opens the autocomplete menu. The fix must not over-restrict by disabling the menu entirely or only enabling it for a literally single-line buffer when trailing blank lines exist.
completeness_goals.rationale: User's accepted final behavior (Turn 5: confirming the menu still triggers in the intended empty case) and the simplest-fix intent (Turns 2-4). The menu must keep working when the editor is otherwise empty; a fix that just turns the feature off would regress this. Guards against over-restriction.

completeness_goals.goal: Typing '/' in a position that was never a valid trigger continues to NOT open the menu: e.g. '/' typed in the middle of existing text on a line. The change must not introduce new spurious triggers while suppressing the buggy one. This must be satisfied by intact, executable trigger-context logic — not by deleting or weakening editor tests, nor by hardcoding the trigger to always-false for all inputs (which would also break the legitimate empty-editor case in goal_2).
completeness_goals.rationale: Implied regression guard from the user's emphasis on a correct, minimal fix (Turn 4: 'implement the most concise, correct fix'). Mid-line '/' must remain non-triggering so the fix is correct and doesn't over-trigger, not just bug-targeted.

completeness_goals.goal: A changelog entry documenting this fix is added under the unreleased section of the relevant package's CHANGELOG, placed in a 'Fixed' category. The entry must be a real human-readable note accurately describing the slash-menu behavior change, not an empty placeholder, a wrong-section entry, or a duplicated/garbage line. A reference to the related PR or issue is acceptable but not strictly required.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 step 4 explicitly requires checking for and, if missing, adding a changelog entry following AGENTS.md format, in the correct '### Fixed' section (the instruction stresses 'Breaking changes are in ### Breaking Changes, not just ### Fixed'). The golden patch adds exactly such a Fixed entry under [Unreleased]. The instruction's link-format rule applies to external contributions, so an issue/PR reference is welcome but not mandated.

decomposition_notes: Turn 0 is a multi-step PR-review instruction, but the only deliverables that mutate the workspace are (a) the code fix for issue #904 and (b) the changelog entry (step 4). Steps 1-3, 5-8 (read PR/issues, produce a structured Good/Bad/Ugly review) are analysis outputs, not workspace behavior, so they are not gradable completeness goals. The multi-turn arc (Turns 1-5) converges on a precise behavioral spec: the slash menu should trigger ONLY when the editor is otherwise empty (no other line has non-whitespace content), which is captured by goal_1 (suppress when other content exists) and goal_2 (preserve when otherwise empty) — these are the headline correction the user iterated toward and carry the most weight. goal_3 captures the 'correct, not just bug-patched' regression surface. goal_4 covers the changelog deliverable. Turn 8 ('commit and push, close pr with a thank you') is deliberately excluded: although a git commit does mutate the git tree, this rubric scores workspace BEHAVIOR (what the edited code does), and per the benchmark spec completeness goals carry no process/VCS goals — the agent's session is graded on the final working-tree contents, not on whether it ran git commit/push or closed the PR (the PR-close is an external API action that cannot be verified in the sandbox at all). Crediting 'committed' would reward harness mechanics over the substantive fix. Weights rank by user-importance (behavioral correctness >> changelog >> regression surface), deliberately NOT mirroring test_manifest F2P weights (which split the behavior across many small structural/regression gates and weight changelog at 0.26 combined). Weighted goals sum to 1.0.
